text: The surgeon observes a grossly distended gallbladder with a green appearance, indicating gangrenous changes, and prepares to decompress it for easier handling.
The surgeon elevates the gallbladder and begins blunt dissection, manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to manage bleeding caused by the dense inflammatory reaction during the procedure, utilizing the instrument as an effective retractor.
The surgeon performs dissection high up on the gallbladder wall, manipulating the gallbladder laterally and medially to facilitate achieving the critical view.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum along the undersurface of the suspected cystic duct within the hepatocystic triangle, performing dissection up the lateral side of the gallbladder on the right.
The surgeon observes the edematous and friable gallbladder wall, noting visible gangrenous changes on the surface.
The surgeon identifies that the dissection is high on the gallbladder wall and proceeds to dig deeper to expose the junction of the cystic duct and the hepatocystic triangle.
The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to improve visibility and opens the peritoneum to elevate the bottom of the gallbladder, bringing the junction of the cystic duct into view.

The surgeon joins the initial peritoneal incision, which was made high up on the gallbladder, using a suction irrigator to maintain visibility and ensure dissection within the proper anatomical plane.

The surgeon clears the hepatocystic triangle, revealing the artery and the junction of the cystic duct with the gallbladder. The bottom of the gallbladder is elevated to achieve the critical view, with the posterior aspect of the cystic duct becoming visible.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to develop a window around the cystic duct, clearing lymphovascular tissue that impedes retraction. The backside of the cystic duct is cleaned, and a window is created in the hepatocystic triangle. A minor arterial bleed occurs during the maneuver, prompting the surgeon to address it.

The surgeon works to achieve a clearer critical view of the cystic duct and artery, using blunt dissection to separate tissue within the hepatocystic triangle. Despite previous bleeding, the surgeon refrains from taking the cystic duct until the view is sufficiently improved.
The surgeon carefully thins out the lymphovascular tissue holding the gallbladder, ensuring there are no ductal or vessel structures present before proceeding with dissection.
The surgeon has achieved a complete critical view, allowing for safe clipping and division of the cystic duct. Following this, the gallbladder is removed from the liver bed.

The surgeon carefully separates posterior vessels from the gallbladder to avoid bleeding, maintaining vigilance while detaching the gallbladder from the liver edge. The surgical field appears smoky, requiring venting to improve visibility.

The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the liver bed, leaving it attached at the top to use as a retractor. Hemostasis is achieved in the liver bed, followed by irrigation. The gallbladder is then amputated and extracted.
